title: Darkroom Facilities and Equipment
narrationScript: Every radiograph is made twice. The exposure in the bay builds an invisible latent image, and the darkroom turns that latent image into the film you grade. A perfect exposure can be ruined in thirty seconds by a leaky door, a fogging safelight, or a dirty bench, so a Level Two technician treats the darkroom as a precision instrument, not a closet. In this lesson we walk through the facility itself and the equipment in it. Start with the room. A darkroom has one job above all others, keeping white light away from unprocessed film. The walls, ceiling, door, and every penetration for plumbing and wiring must be light tight. Entrances are designed so people can come and go without flooding the room, either an interlocked single door, a double door vestibule where one door cannot open until the other is closed, a revolving light tight door, or a maze style light trap that lets you walk in with no door at all. Ventilation matters too, because processing chemistry puts fumes in the air and people work in there for hours. The vents are light trapped so air moves but light does not. To prove the room is tight, go inside, shut everything, and let your eyes adapt for ten to fifteen minutes. Then look slowly around the door frame, the ceiling fixtures, and every pipe penetration. Any glow you can see, film can see better, because film integrates light over time. The layout splits into a dry side and a wet side, and the separation is absolute. The dry side holds the loading bench, the film bin, and the cassettes. The wet side holds the processing tanks or the processor, the sink, and the chemistry. Film and chemicals never share a surface, because one fingerprint of fixer on a loading bench will mark every film that touches it. Next, the choice between manual and automatic processing. Manual processing uses a line of tanks, developer, stop bath, fixer, and wash, with the film carried on hangers and timed by the technician using the time and temperature method. It is cheap to set up, easy to maintain, and flexible, but every variable belongs to the operator, and a busy or careless operator produces films that wander in density and contrast. An automatic processor pulls the film through rollers at a fixed speed through heated chemistry and a dryer, and hands you a dry radiograph in roughly eight to twelve minutes for a typical industrial machine. The machine wins on speed, throughput, and above all consistency, but it costs more, demands regular cleaning and maintenance, and it will repeat a chemistry problem on every film until somebody notices. Now safelights. A safelight is not safe, it is only slow. It is a low wattage lamp behind a filter chosen for the film's spectral sensitivity. Most industrial x-ray films are sensitive to blue light, and some to green, so safelight filters pass only the deep red and amber light the film barely responds to. The classic rule for direct lighting is a fifteen watt bulb at least four feet from the working surface. Indirect fixtures that bounce light off the ceiling can run somewhat brighter. Even a correct safelight will fog film if you give it time, so handle film quickly and keep boxes and bins closed. Prove your safelights with a fog test. Lay a piece of film on the bench, put a few coins on it, leave it out for the longest time film would normally be handled, then process it. If you can see the outline of a coin, the bench is not safe, and typical practice limits any added safelight fog to about five hundredths of density. The room also needs viewing light. A wet viewing illuminator near the tanks lets you check that an image came out before you release the setup, while final reading happens on a high intensity variable viewer in the viewing area. Remember the math of density. A density of two passes one percent of the light, a density of three passes one tenth of one percent, and a density of four passes only one part in ten thousand, so viewers must be intensely bright, diffuse, masked to the film, and cooled. Finally the working gear. A loading bench that is smooth, clean, dry, and used for nothing else. A light tight film bin. Interval timers you can read in the dark. Accurate thermometers, never mercury, because a broken bulb contaminates the chemistry and creates a hazard. Film hangers kept clean and dry, separate stirring paddles for each tank, floating lids on the tanks, a tempered water supply, and a dust free drying cabinet. None of it is glamorous. All of it shows up in your image quality.
imagePrompt: A working film darkroom under deep red safelight, a technician standing between a clean stainless loading bench with film cassettes on one side and a row of stainless processing tanks on the other.

sections.heading: The Darkroom Is Part of the Imaging Chain
sections.body:
A radiograph is made twice - once in the exposure bay, where radiation builds an invisible latent image, and again in the darkroom, where chemistry makes it visible and permanent. A perfect exposure can be destroyed in seconds by a light leak, a fogging safelight, or a contaminated bench, so the facility is treated as part of the imaging system and verified like one.

The room must be completely **light-tight**: walls, ceiling, door, and every plumbing and electrical penetration. Entrances are designed so people can pass without flooding the room with white light - an interlocked single door, a double-door vestibule in which one door cannot open until the other closes, a revolving light-tight door, or a doorless maze whose overlapping walls trap light while letting people walk straight through. Ventilation is required - processing chemistry puts acetic and sulfite fumes into the air - and the ducts carry light-trapped louvers so air moves and light does not.

Prove the room before trusting it. Step inside, close everything, and let your eyes dark-adapt for ten to fifteen minutes. Then inspect the door frame, ceiling fixtures, hatch edges, and every penetration. Any glow you can see, film sees better - film integrates light over its whole handling time.

sections.caption: Plan view of a working darkroom: light-trap entrance, separated dry and wet sides, safelights over the work areas, pass-through hatch, and light-trapped ventilation.

sections.heading: Manual Versus Automatic Processing
sections.body:
The wet side is built around one of two choices. **Manual processing** uses a line of insulated tanks - developer, stop bath, fixer, and wash - with films carried on hangers and timed by the technician using the time-temperature method. It is inexpensive, simple to maintain, and flexible for odd film sizes and low volume, but every variable belongs to the operator: time, temperature, agitation, and replenishment. Careless habits show up directly as density and contrast that wander from film to film. Counting a full wash and drying, a manual film takes an hour or more dry-to-dry.

An **automatic processor** pulls film through roller racks at fixed speed through heated developer, fixer, and wash, then through a dryer - a dry radiograph in roughly **8-12 minutes** for a typical industrial machine. Time and temperature are locked by the transport speed and thermostat, and replenishment is metered automatically, which is why processors win on consistency and throughput. The costs are capital, plumbing and power, scheduled cleaning, and the fact that a sick processor repeats its fault on every film until the operator notices. Both approaches demand quality control; neither forgives neglect.

sections.caption: Manual versus automatic processing - what you trade.
rows: Low volume, remote or field darkrooms
One fault repeated on every film

sections.heading: Safelights
sections.body:
A safelight is **slow, not safe**. It is a low-wattage lamp behind a filter chosen for the film's spectral sensitivity: most industrial X-ray films are blue-sensitive (some green-sensitive), so safelight filters pass only the deep red to amber light the film barely responds to. Classic amber-brown filters of the 6B type suit blue-sensitive films; dark-red GBX-type filters cover both blue- and green-sensitive films and are the conservative choice. A cracked or faded filter leaks white light - inspect filters on a schedule and replace them without debate.

The traditional rule for direct fixtures is a **15-watt bulb at least 4 ft (1.2 m)** from the working surface. Indirect fixtures that bounce light off the ceiling may use somewhat brighter bulbs (about 25 W) because the light arrives diffused and weakened. Even a correct safelight fogs film given enough time, so keep handling brisk, keep the film bin closed, and never leave film lying out on the bench.

Prove the installation with a **fog test**: lay a sheet of film on the bench, place a few coins on it, leave it exposed to the safelights for the longest normal handling time, then process it. A visible coin outline means the safelights are fogging film. A typical acceptance is no more than about **0.05 added density**.

sections.caption: Direct safelights use a 15 W bulb behind a filter matched to the film, at least 4 ft above the bench; the coin test proves the installation is not fogging film.

sections.title: A safelight is slow, not safe
sections.body: Safelight tolerance is **time-limited**. Film fogs under any safelight eventually, and that fog adds straight to base-plus-fog - which codes typically cap around **0.30 density** for the processed, unexposed film. Keep handling time short, keep boxes and bins closed, run the coin test on a schedule, and replace cracked or faded filters immediately.

sections.heading: Viewer Lights
sections.body:
Two kinds of viewing light serve the darkroom. A **wet-viewing illuminator** near the wash lets you confirm an image came out before the exposure setup is released - useful, but never the basis for final interpretation, because a wet, undried film differs optically from the finished record. Final reading happens in the viewing area on a **high-intensity, variable illuminator**.

The physics dictates the hardware. Density is logarithmic - D equals log base 10 of incident over transmitted light - so a density of 2.0 passes **1%** of the viewer's light, 3.0 passes **0.1%**, and 4.0 passes **0.01%**, one part in ten thousand. Since code-quality radiographs run to density 4.0 and beyond, the illuminator must be intensely bright, with variable output so thin films do not blind you, a diffusing screen for even luminance, and masks to block glare around the film edges - stray light around a dark film destroys your ability to see into it. High-luminance **spot viewers** serve the densest areas. Powerful lamps run hot, so viewers are fan-cooled; a failed fan shortens lamp life and can warp a film left on the screen. View in a darkened area with your eyes adapted.

sections.heading: Loading Bench and Miscellaneous Equipment
sections.body:
The **loading bench** anchors the dry side: smooth, non-porous, clean, dry, grounded against static where the climate demands it, and used for nothing except film, screens, cassettes, and holders. No chemistry crosses it - one fingerprint of fixer marks every film that touches the spot. Under or beside it sits a **light-tight film bin** for opened stock (lead-lined where radiation is nearby), and many darkrooms add an interlocked **pass-through hatch** so loaded cassettes move to the exposure side without opening the room.

The small equipment earns its keep:

- **Interval timers** with luminous faces you can read in the dark.
- **Accurate thermometers** - alcohol, bimetal dial, or digital. Mercury thermometers are banned from darkrooms: one broken bulb contaminates the chemistry and creates a toxic cleanup.
- **Film hangers** (channel and clip types) kept clean, dry, and uncorroded - crusted hangers cause streaks and kinked corners.
- **Separate stirring paddles** for each tank, never interchanged.
- **Floating lids and dust covers** to slow developer oxidation.
- A **tempered-water mixing valve** to hold wash and water-jacket temperatures.
- A **drying cabinet** with filtered, heated airflow.
- A **densitometer with a calibrated step tablet**, plus a room thermometer-hygrometer, to keep the whole system honest.

figures.title: Safelight rules and the coin fog test

checkQuestions.question: Compared with a manual tank line, the principal advantage of an automatic film processor is:
options: It eliminates the need for processing quality control
Consistent, repeatable processing, with time and temperature controlled by the machine
It reaches higher film densities than manual development can
It removes the need for safelights in the darkroom
checkQuestions.explanation: Transport speed and thermostatic control lock development time and temperature, so films match from morning to night. Safelights and daily quality control are still required.

checkQuestions.question: Which setup satisfies the traditional rule for a direct safelight over a loading bench?
options: A 60 W bulb at 2 ft behind any red glass
A 15 W bulb behind a filter matched to the film's spectral sensitivity, at least 4 ft from the work surface
Any bulb wattage, provided film is handled in under ten minutes
An unfiltered 25 W bulb aimed at the ceiling
checkQuestions.explanation: Direct safelighting is traditionally limited to about 15 W behind a proper filter at 4 ft (1.2 m) or more; indirect bounce fixtures may run somewhat brighter, but never unfiltered.

checkQuestions.question: A safelight fog test film comes back showing a clearly visible lighter outline where a coin sat. The correct conclusion is:
options: The film stock was outdated and should be discarded
The developer was exhausted and under-developed the film
The safelights fogged the film, and the installation must be corrected before film is handled there
The coin contaminated the emulsion with metal salts
checkQuestions.explanation: The coin shielded the film beneath it while safelight fog exposed the surrounding area; a visible outline means the safelight wattage, distance, filter, or handling time is unsafe.

checkQuestions.question: A radiograph reads density 3.0 in the area of interest. What fraction of the viewer's light reaches your eye through that area?
checkQuestions.explanation: Density is log base 10 of incident over transmitted light, so D = 3.0 transmits 1/1000 of the light, which is 0.1% - the reason high-intensity illuminators are required.

checkQuestions.question: Which item belongs on the darkroom's dry side?
options: The light-tight film bin beneath the loading bench
checkQuestions.explanation: The dry side holds only film, screens, cassettes, and the bin that stores opened stock; all chemistry, tanks, and recovery equipment live on the wet side so contamination never reaches unprocessed film.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality 2.1-2.1.5 (darkroom facilities and equipment)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2 (radiographic examination - processing and viewing, general practice)
Film manufacturer darkroom design and safelight guidance (reference practice)
